text: The preoperative bronchoscopy demonstrated the endobronchial lesion.
The carotid is to our right.
The endobronchial lesion is to the left.
And we have at least three tracheal rings or three bronchial rings between the carotid and the endobronchial lesion.
This is the interoperative bronchoscopy, which demonstrates again the carina to our right and the endobronchial lesion to our left.
We tried to use a double lumen right-sided tube, but we were unable to get it positioned to ventilate the left lung and not the right lung.
So we ended up using just a single lumen tube and doing the operation utilizing ECMO.
This is the ECMO cannula in the right internal jugular vein.
And as you can see, it's draped around his head coming off the patient's left side and it'll go down by the feet.
We mark out where our ports go and the anterior port is going to be at the costal margin of the seventh intercostal space.
We put a 12 millimeter port here so that we could staple the ascus vein to get it out of the way.
In the mid axillary line, eighth intercostal space, an eight millimeter port is used for the camera.
Eight centimeters more posterior, another eight millimeter port used.
for a caudier, and then six centimeters more posterior, another eight millimeter port is used for the tip-up fenestrated grasper.
Over the 10th rib and over the diaphragm, a 12 millimeter, 100 millimeter air seal assistant port was used.
And this demonstrates the ports in place, the patient's head's to the left, feet to the right, and this is the lateral decubitus position.
We'll start off with a paravertebral intercostal nerve block with expirill.
We then mobilize inferior pulmonary ligament.
We retract the lung anteriorly and we dissect out completely the subcarinal space.
This patient had several large subcoronal lymph nodes, which on final pathology were negative.
But we do try to spare what bronchioles we can.
But in this space, it's more important to have an open space to facilitate your anastomosis.
That's a left main stem bronchus that's being dissected.
This is another subcoronal lymph node, which is being dissected.
And we dissect the left main stem bronchus down to the left upper lobe bronchus.
And we try to do it circumferentially.
So you can see we're going around the bronchus here, doing our dissection.
And again, you can see that we're circumferentially around it.
So it's totally freed up and we're ready for the resection.
But we decided to, first of all, go ahead and immobilize the trachea.
We dissect out the azagus vein and we actually resected the azagus.
We divided it as seen here.
near the superior vena cava, and then we'll do it near the spine.
You could leave it long and take the stump and put it on the anastomosis, but we decided to use an intercostal muscle flap instead.
And again, we've got the distal trachea mobilized, and the whole left main stone bronchus is mobilized.
So now we're going to mobilize our intercostal
muscle flap.
I use the long bipolar grasper and I go off the superior portion of the lower rib and the inferior portion of the up rib.
When you're underneath the rib where we're dissecting here, we're real careful for the intercostal bundle because we want to spare the perfusion to the muscle flap.
So as we're dissecting, we eventually, we're looking down, we're eventually going to shift to go up, which we have done.
And then we're going to switch over to the spatula cautery, which facilitates taking it down.
We're dividing the intercostal muscle flap.
Usually you can just cauterize through the intercostal artery and vein.
Sometimes you have to clip it.
But once you have divided it, you can just pull down on your muscle flap, separate it from the chest wall.
And you can see that the spatula cautery facilitates this.
And it's coming down quite nicely.
We've mobilized it down near the spine.
We think we've got adequate length to go around the anastomosis.
We now are going to cut into the left main stone bronchus just at its origin at the carina.
And you can see the endobronchial mass.
These are the cautery shears.
We're just using the cutting portion of it.
If we had much bleeding, we could cauterize it if we wanted, but this is just going along, freeing up the tissue.
And this is the, the,

proximal margin that we send off for a frozen section now we're going to resect the specimen distally we're coming across and you can see the specimen within the left main stone bronchus that's freeing it up now it's totally freed up again we're going to cut some that's putting it back in place and we're going to cut some for our distal margin and we also send this off for frozen section and before we do anastomosis we make sure that that is free of any cancer
We put stay sutures on the distal left main and we bring it out through the chest to pull it up to facilitate the anastomosis.
And again, we do it on either side and you can see how it kind of pulls it up to help us with the anastomosis.
We put the specimen in a small specimen bag and externalize.
We use a 3-0 monofilament V-lock suture that's absorbable.
So we're going to do the cartilaginous portion first, which is down or more posterior to us, and save the membranous portion at the end.
So now we're going inside out, outside in, all forehand sutures.
You can see it goes quite nicely.
There's no tension on it.
And as we go through, we'll pull up the suture to bring the anastomosis together.
I usually will maintain a Cartier in my left hand so I can manipulate the bronchus as well as opposed to a DeBakey.
And we're coming around.
And once we get to the other side of the cartilaginous portion, I will bring in another V-lock stitch to complete it.
And again, we pull it up.
Sometimes we won't pull on the suture.
We'll just push it or pulley it.
And again, we're coming through, we're getting near the membranous portion now.
Again, we're going to tighten it up.
We're bringing in another suture and we'll come through and pass it through our little loop.
Then we'll do another throw.
Then we're going to end up tying these two Phelox sutures together.
So this is the other additional throw.
So now we're going to secure these two sutures together.
We cut the needle off and externalized it.
And now we're going to complete our anastomosis.
Again, it's all forehand, excellent vision.
You can see what the robot does.
It gives you superior vision and stability.
ECMO provides you a motionless feel and the lung is out of your way.
We did give 5,000 units of heparin and did not have to redose, maintaining an ACT around 150 to 200.
It's a little bit oozy, but it's not excessive and it's certainly tolerable.
And again, this is getting near the completion.
You have a little dog ear that we're going to tuck.
And that kind of completes our anastomosis.
And we'll cut it and get the needle out.
We're going to now bring our intercostal muscle flap.
We need to lengthen it a little bit so you can incise the fascia that you have.
And that will give you adequate length to get all the way around the left main stone bronchus and the anastomosis.
You can see it comes around quite nicely.
We're going to spray this, the top side, with a little Pro-Gel.
Just ensure that you won't have an air leak.
That is Progel.
Now we're going to secure the muscle flap to the bronchus to ensure that it stays in its designed place.
And this pretty much completes the anastomosis in the rotational intercostal muscle flap.
We put a 20 French chest tube and we put it anteriorly, a 24 blank posteriorly.
We fill the chest with saline.
We test for an air leak.
There is no air leak.
And we watch the lung inflate under direct vision.
This is the proximal bronchus that we excised, and this is the distal, and that's the specimen in place.
At the very conclusion, we did a bronchoscopy just to look at the anastomosis, and you can see the anastomosis looks nice.
